references.title: A Self-Attentive model for Knowledge Tracing

extracted.Student Performance Definition: Student is able to solve exercise at step t+1
extracted.Students: 4000 Synthetic learners, 4417 ASSISTments09 learners, 19917 ASSISTments15 learners, 686 ASSISTmentsChall, 333 Statics learners
extracted.Flaw of Previous Models: DKT approaches use either RNNs or RNN-derived structures which makes thems vulnerable to deal with sparser datasets and/or longer sequences.
extracted.Novelty of Model: The authors introduce a Transformer architecture which, at the time, was the state-of-the-art form of addressing sequential data.
extracted.Preprocessing Details:
The model involves 2 forms of embeddings: A question embedding and an interaction embedding.

The authors also employ a normalization layer at the end of each layer: be it feed-forward or self-attention layer.
extracted.Comments:
Authors also perform an Ablation study:

The loss in performance coming from positional encoding is not very pronounced.

Results show that not only model performs better but is also1 ordder of magnitude faster.
extracted.Limitations:
No Cross-Validation.

Could explore more features and data from other sources.

annotations.text: Knowledge Tracing estimates the performance of a student in different learning activities, allowing it to model the student’s mastery of different knowledge concepts. The current state-of-the-art methods are BKT, DKT and Dynamic Key-value Mmory Networks and all have the issue of poor generalization in the rpesence of sparse data.

The authors propose an approach that estimates Knowledge Components from past activities (sort of using preceding knowledge components to estimate current ones?).  This approach is Self-Attention Based (SAKT). Results on multiple datasets show that SAKT beats other alternatives by an average 4.43% on the AUC score.
